turns.content: I'm getting this error: 'error: failed to parse manifest at ...: profile.bench is not valid. Did you mean release?'. I tried adding a custom profile but it looks like cargo just rejects unknown profiles entirely.

That error happens because Cargo only recognizes specific named profiles by default, and if you define a new one without the right syntax or inheritance, it fails to parse. You need to explicitly inherit from an existing profile like release using the inherit attribute on your custom profile definition.
So I can't just add arbitrary settings? Like setting opt-level and overflow-checks independently?

You cannot define a completely standalone profile without inheritance in Cargo's manifest. Every custom profile must specify inherit = "release" or another existing profile to ensure compatibility with the resolver. However, once you do that, you can override specific fields like opt-level and overflow-checks for your new profile.
Okay, so I need a [profile.bench] section inheriting from release but with lower optimization?

Exactly. You define [profile.bench] and set inherit = "release" to copy over default settings like lib-features and rustflags, then override opt-level to something like 3 or add overflow-checks = false if you want faster benchmarks without runtime checks.
Wait, what about debug builds? Can I have a separate profile for CI that runs faster?

Yes. You can create [profile.ci] inheriting from release but setting opt-level to 0 or even higher than normal release depending on your needs, and disable overflow-checks if you don't need them for CI speed. This lets you tune performance per environment without breaking the default build.
But what about the features? Do I have to list them all manually?

No. When you inherit from release, it automatically includes the default feature set unless you explicitly disable some with negative feature flags like --features = "foo -bar" in your command line or define overrides in the profile section.
So I can just copy the release block and tweak opt-level?
Not quite. You must include inherit = "release" in your profile definition, otherwise cargo will treat it as an unknown profile type and fail to parse the manifest entirely.
Ah! I was thinking of profiles like custom build scripts or something else. So this is just a configuration thing in Cargo.toml?

Yes, it's purely manifest configuration using the profile table syntax. Once you get that right with inherit, cargo will recognize your [profile.bench] or any other custom profile and apply those settings during compilation.
Okay so I can now write a valid [profile.ci] with opt-level = 0 to make CI runs faster without breaking builds?

That's exactly what you want. You define [profile.ci] inheriting from release, set opt-level to your desired value like 0 or 1, and skip overflow-checks if needed. Now cargo will respect that profile when you run --profile ci.

I have a benchmark suite running on our production cluster and the current build is taking too long because it's compiling with debug flags. I'm using serde_json to serialize the output, but the compiler seems to be optimizing aggressively even though we need full debug info for some assertions. Is there a better way to handle this?

You can define a custom profile called [profile.bench] in your Cargo.toml that inherits from the release profile but sets overflow-checks to yes and disables panic hooks, which reduces startup time while keeping safety checks. Would you like me to show how to structure this?

I tried defining [profile.custom] with opt-level 3 and debug-assertions false but the benchmarks still fail because some internal tests need overflow checks enabled. Can I selectively enable those without breaking everything else?

Yes, you can create separate profile entries like [profile.ci-fast] that sets opt-level to 2 and disables debug assertions for CI speed while keeping a dedicated [profile.debug-full] with overflow-checks yes and panic hooks enabled. Each profile is independent so they don't interfere.
Wait, I thought custom profiles only inherit defaults from the base profile? If I define multiple profiles like [profile.bench] and [profile.ci], won't they conflict when cargo runs the build?

No, cargo treats each profile as a separate compilation target. When you use --profile bench it only compiles with those settings, so there is no conflict between [profile.ci] and [profile.bench]. They are independent configurations.

Okay, but I still can't get the overflow-checks to apply selectively. Is there a way to use profile inheritance so my main release profile gets default settings while only specific modules override it?

You cannot target individual crates or modules with profiles in cargo; each crate is built entirely under one profile at a time. The solution is to define [profile.ci-fast] and [profile.bench] explicitly and run them separately via cargo build --profile ci-fast or cargo bench, avoiding the need for selective overrides.

I'm reviewing our CI configuration and need to understand the difference between a standard release profile and a custom optimized build profile in Cargo. My current scripts just use `--release` but I suspect we're missing something about how profiles actually inherit settings.

You are correct that the default release profile is a solid baseline, but it doesn't allow you to override specific flags like overflow checks or debug assertions without breaking your build script logic. When you define a custom profile in `Cargo.toml`, it inherits from the parent profile (usually `release`) by default, so you can keep the high optimization levels while selectively disabling `overflow-checks` or `debug-assertions`. This is crucial for CI pipelines where you want maximum compile speed without paying the runtime penalty of runtime checks that don't matter in automated testing environments.

That makes sense regarding inheritance, but I'm confused about how exactly we specify these flags. If I define a new profile named `ci`, does it automatically start with the release settings, or do I have to manually copy every setting over?

You don't need to manually copy every setting; by default, a custom profile inherits all fields from its parent, which is typically `release`. You only need to override the specific flags you want to change, such as setting `opt-level` to `3` for maximum speed while keeping `lto = true`, or explicitly setting `overflow-checks = false` and `debug-assertions = false`. This means your CI build will run significantly faster than a standard release because it avoids the overhead of runtime checks that slow down compilation in automated environments.

Imagine I'm writing a benchmark suite for my new crate and I want to run tests with the same optimization level as release but without the debug symbols. Can I just use the default profile settings or do I need to define something in Cargo.toml?

You don't need to manually set everything, but you should define a custom build profile like [profile.bench] in your Cargo.toml to ensure consistency across runs. This allows you to specify exact opt-level flags and other settings for that specific profile without affecting default compilation.
Okay so I add the section under profiles but what exactly are those fields doing? Do they completely override release settings or just apply on top of them?

Those fields don't fully replace the defaults; instead, they inherit from the parent profile which is usually release. You can then selectively change values like opt-level or overflow-checks while keeping other settings inherited.
Wait, I thought profiles were completely separate so if I define a new one it would just be an entirely different compilation path with no relationship to release?

That's a common misconception. Profiles are designed to inherit from each other by default; you can set [profile.bench] to inherit from [profile.release] and only override specific fields like opt-level or debug assertions.
So if I don't specify any fields in the profile section it just copies all values from release automatically?

Exactly, when you define a custom profile without explicitly setting keys, Cargo copies those settings from whichever parent profile is configured. This makes tweaking profiles much easier since you only need to change what matters for your specific use case.
Got it so I can create a fast CI build profile that inherits from release but disables overflow checks and lowers the optimization level?
Yes, that's exactly right. You'd define [profile.ci] inheriting from release and then set lower opt-level values or disable debug assertions to speed up builds without breaking your code logic.
One last thing I'm confused about - does setting profile flags in Cargo.toml actually affect the build output or is it just a configuration change?

The flags you set directly influence how cargo compiles your crate, changing the resulting binary's characteristics like size and performance. This is how you get different build artifacts tailored for benchmarks versus development environments.

Imagine we need to run a benchmark suite where timing matters more than debug symbols, and then later we want a CI profile that builds faster but still catches overflow errors. How do I define these two separate profiles in Cargo so they inherit from release but tweak the specific flags for each use case?

You can define custom profiles by adding sections under [profile] in your Cargo.toml file. For the benchmark suite, you would create a profile named bench that inherits from the default release profile using the inherit = "release" attribute, then override opt-level to 3 and set overflow-checks to false since speed is the priority there. For the CI profile, you could define a new section like ci-profile that also sets inherit = "release", keeps overflow-checks enabled as true to catch runtime panics, but reduces opt-level to 2 for better compile times without sacrificing too much performance compared to debug mode.

That clarifies the inheritance mechanism, but I'm confused about how these profiles interact with the actual binary compilation when running cargo build --profile bench. Does this create a completely separate binary file or does it modify the existing release artifact?

When you run cargo build --profile bench, Cargo generates a distinct output directory named target/release/bench so the artifacts never collide with your standard release binary. The compiler processes the source code once but applies the specific flag set from that profile definition to generate the executable in that separate folder. This means you can safely mix them: your main application uses the default or release profile, while test suites and CI pipelines pull from their own isolated profile directories without interfering with each other.
Okay so if I want to compare performance between these two profiles without rebuilding everything twice, is there a way to run cargo bench directly that automatically handles this?

Yes, you can use the cargo-bench command which is part of the benchmarking ecosystem designed specifically for Rust projects. This tool reads your Cargo.toml and understands how to invoke cargo with different profile flags internally so you don't have to manually type the full build command every time. It effectively wraps the underlying cargo invocation to run tests against both the standard release profile and your custom bench profile in sequence, then aggregates the timing results into a single report.

I see how that helps with workflow, but I still want to understand why we bother separating these profiles at all instead of just using one optimized build for everything. What's the fundamental reason behind keeping opt-level and overflow-checks distinct?

The fundamental reason is that different environments have conflicting priorities: production systems need maximum speed with minimal checks, while CI pipelines often run on slower machines where compile time dominates runtime performance, yet they still cannot afford silent overflows in their test suites. By separating them into distinct profiles using inherit = "release", you decouple these competing constraints so your release binary remains fast and safe for users, while your CI builds remain efficient enough to complete quickly without sacrificing the safety net of overflow checks that prevents subtle bugs from slipping through.
